title: Validate Binary Search Tree
description:
Given an integer array `tree` representing a binary tree in **level-order (BFS) order**, determine if it is a valid **Binary Search Tree (BST)**.

A valid BST is defined as follows:
- The left subtree of a node contains only nodes with values **strictly less than** the node's value.
- The right subtree of a node contains only nodes with values **strictly greater than** the node's value.
- Both the left and right subtrees must also be valid BSTs.

The array uses `-1` to represent `null` (missing) nodes in the level-order encoding.

**Example:**
```
tree = [5, 3, 7, 1, 4, 6, 8]
```
This represents:
```
    5
   / \
  3   7
 / \ / \
1  4 6  8
```
Every left child is smaller, every right child is larger → valid BST → `true`

**Note:** Node values can be negative. The constraint `-1` is **only** used for null placeholders, so actual node values will never be `-1`.
constraints: 1 <= tree.length <= 10000
Node values are unique integers in the range [-10^4, 10^4] (excluding -1, which denotes null)
The tree array represents a complete binary tree structure in level-order
Expected time complexity: O(n)
Expected auxiliary space: O(n)

params.name: tree
params.description: Level-order (BFS) representation of the binary tree, with -1 for null nodes

examples.explanation: The tree is a valid BST: left subtree values (1,3,4) are all less than 5, right subtree values (6,7,8) are all greater than 5, and this property holds recursively.
The root is 5, but its right child is 4, which is less than 5. This violates the BST property, so the answer is false.
Node 6 is in the right subtree of root 10, but 6 < 10. This violates the BST property.

title: Search in a Binary Search Tree
description:
Given an integer array `tree` representing a **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in level-order (BFS) order, and an integer `val`, find the node whose value equals `val` and return that value. If no such node exists, return `-1`.

The array uses `-1` to represent `null` (missing) nodes. Node values will never be `-1`.

**Example:**
```
tree = [4, 2, 7, 1, 3, -1, -1]
val = 2
```
The BST looks like:
```
    4
   / \
  2   7
 / \
1   3
```
Node with value `2` exists → return `2`.

If `val = 5`, no such node exists → return `-1`.

**Hint:** Use the BST property to guide your search: if `val < current`, go left; if `val > current`, go right.
constraints: 1 <= tree.length <= 10000
Node values are unique integers in the range [0, 10^4] (excluding -1, which denotes null)
val is an integer in the range [0, 10^4]
Expected time complexity: O(h) where h is the height of the BST
Expected auxiliary space: O(h)

params.name: tree
params.description: Level-order (BFS) representation of the BST, with -1 for null nodes

params.name: val
params.description: The value to search for in the BST

examples.explanation: The node with value 2 exists in the BST, so we return 2.
No node with value 5 exists in the BST, so we return -1.

title: Minimum Value in BST
description:
Given an integer array `tree` representing a **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in level-order (BFS) order, return the **minimum value** stored in the BST.

The array uses `-1` to represent `null` (missing) nodes. Node values will never be `-1`.

**Key Insight:** In a BST, the minimum value is always located at the **leftmost node** — keep going left until there is no more left child.

**Example:**
```
tree = [8, 3, 10, 1, 6, -1, 14]
```
The BST looks like:
```
      8
     / \
    3   10
   / \    \
  1   6   14
```
The leftmost node is `1` → return `1`.
constraints: 1 <= tree.length <= 10000
Node values are integers in the range [0, 10^4] (excluding -1, which denotes null)
The input is guaranteed to be a valid BST
Expected time complexity: O(h) where h is the height of the BST
Expected auxiliary space: O(1)

params.name: tree
params.description: Level-order (BFS) representation of the BST, with -1 for null nodes

examples.explanation: Following the leftmost path: 8 → 3 → 1. Node 1 has no left child, so 1 is the minimum.
The leftmost node is 3, which is the minimum value in the BST.

title: Maximum Value in BST
description:
Given an integer array `tree` representing a **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in level-order (BFS) order, return the **maximum value** stored in the BST.

The array uses `-1` to represent `null` (missing) nodes. Node values will never be `-1`.

**Key Insight:** In a BST, the maximum value is always located at the **rightmost node** — keep going right until there is no more right child.

**Example:**
```
tree = [8, 3, 10, 1, 6, -1, 14]
```
The BST looks like:
```
      8
     / \
    3   10
   / \    \
  1   6   14
```
The rightmost node is `14` → return `14`.
constraints: 1 <= tree.length <= 10000
Node values are integers in the range [0, 10^4] (excluding -1, which denotes null)
The input is guaranteed to be a valid BST
Expected time complexity: O(h) where h is the height of the BST
Expected auxiliary space: O(1)

params.name: tree
params.description: Level-order (BFS) representation of the BST, with -1 for null nodes

examples.explanation: Following the rightmost path: 8 → 10 → 14. Node 14 has no right child, so 14 is the maximum.
The rightmost node is 7, which is the maximum value in the BST.

title: Insert into a Binary Search Tree
description:
Given an integer array `tree` representing a **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in level-order (BFS) order and an integer `val`, insert `val` into the BST and return the **level-order (BFS) array** of the resulting tree.

The array uses `-1` to represent `null` (missing) nodes. It is guaranteed that `val` does not already exist in the BST.

**Rules for insertion:**
- If `val` is less than the current node, insert into the **left subtree**.
- If `val` is greater than the current node, insert into the **right subtree**.
- Insert at the first available `null` position following BST rules.

The returned array should represent the tree in level-order with `-1` for null nodes. Trim any trailing `-1`s from the output.

**Example:**
```
tree = [4, 2, 7, 1, 3, -1, -1]
val = 5
```
Insert 5: 5 < 7 (go left of 7), 7's left is null → place 5 there.
```
    4
   / \
  2   7
 / \ /
1  3 5
```
Result: `[4, 2, 7, 1, 3, 5, -1]`
constraints: 1 <= tree.length <= 10000
Node values and val are unique integers in the range [0, 10^4] (excluding -1, which denotes null)
val does not exist in the original tree
The input is guaranteed to be a valid BST
Expected time complexity: O(h) where h is the height of the BST
Expected auxiliary space: O(n)

params.name: tree
params.description: Level-order (BFS) representation of the BST, with -1 for null nodes

params.name: val
params.description: The value to insert into the BST

examples.explanation: 5 > 4, go right to 7. 5 < 7, go left. 7's left is null, so insert 5 there. The resulting level-order array is [4, 2, 7, 1, 3, 5, -1].
25 < 40 → go left to 20. 25 > 20 → go right to 30. 25 < 30 → go left of 30, which is null. Insert 25 as the left child of 30.

title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
description:
Given an integer array `tree` representing a **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in level-order (BFS) order and an integer `k`, return the **k-th smallest value** (1-indexed) among all node values in the BST.

The array uses `-1` to represent `null` (missing) nodes. Node values will never be `-1`.

**Key Insight:** An **inorder traversal** (left → root → right) of a BST yields values in **sorted ascending order**. The k-th element of this sorted sequence is the answer.

**Example:**
```
tree = [3, 1, 4, -1, 2, -1, -1]
k = 1
```
The BST looks like:
```
    3
   / \
  1   4
   \
    2
```
Inorder traversal: `[1, 2, 3, 4]` → 1st smallest = `1`.
constraints: 1 <= tree.length <= 10000
Node values are unique integers in the range [0, 10^4] (excluding -1, which denotes null)
1 <= k <= number of nodes in the tree
The input is guaranteed to be a valid BST
Expected time complexity: O(n)
Expected auxiliary space: O(n)

params.name: tree
params.description: Level-order (BFS) representation of the BST, with -1 for null nodes

params.name: k
params.description: The rank (1-indexed) of the smallest element to find

examples.explanation: Inorder traversal gives [1, 2, 3, 4]. The 1st smallest element is 1.
Inorder traversal gives [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]. The 3rd smallest element is 3.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST
description:
Given an integer array `tree` representing a **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in level-order (BFS) order, and two integers `p` and `q` representing the values of two nodes, return the **value of their Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA)**.

The **Lowest Common Ancestor** of two nodes `p` and `q` in a tree is the deepest node that is an ancestor of both `p` and `q` (a node is a descendant of itself).

The array uses `-1` to represent `null` (missing) nodes. It is guaranteed that both `p` and `q` exist in the tree.

**Key Insight:** Use the BST property:
- If both `p` and `q` are **less than** the current node → LCA is in the **left subtree**.
- If both `p` and `q` are **greater than** the current node → LCA is in the **right subtree**.
- Otherwise → the current node **is** the LCA.

**Example:**
```
tree = [6, 2, 8, 0, 4, 7, 9, -1, -1, 3, 5]
p = 2, q = 8
```
LCA of 2 and 8 is `6` (the root), since one is in the left subtree and the other in the right.
constraints: 1 <= tree.length <= 10000
Node values are unique integers in the range [0, 10^4] (excluding -1, which denotes null)
p and q exist in the BST and p != q
The input is guaranteed to be a valid BST
Expected time complexity: O(h) where h is the height of the BST
Expected auxiliary space: O(1)

params.name: tree
params.description: Level-order (BFS) representation of the BST, with -1 for null nodes

params.name: p
params.description: The value of the first node

params.name: q
params.description: The value of the second node

examples.explanation: 2 is in the left subtree of 6 and 8 is in the right subtree. Since they split at 6, the LCA is 6.
Both 2 and 4 are in the left subtree. At node 2, we see that 4 is in the right subtree of 2. Since they split at 2, the LCA is 2.

title: Inorder Traversal of BST
description:
Given an integer array `tree` representing a **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in level-order (BFS) order, return the **inorder traversal** of the tree as an integer array.

The array uses `-1` to represent `null` (missing) nodes. Node values will never be `-1`.

**Inorder traversal** visits nodes in the order: **left → root → right**.

**Key property:** The inorder traversal of a BST always yields node values in **sorted ascending order**.

**Example:**
```
tree = [4, 2, 6, 1, 3, 5, 7]
```
The BST:
```
      4
     / \
    2   6
   / \ / \
  1  3 5  7
```
Inorder: left of 4 → [1, 2, 3], then root 4, then right of 4 → [5, 6, 7]
Result: `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]`
constraints: 1 <= tree.length <= 10000
Node values are integers in the range [0, 10^4] (excluding -1, which denotes null)
The input is guaranteed to be a valid BST
Expected time complexity: O(n)
Expected auxiliary space: O(n)

params.name: tree
params.description: Level-order (BFS) representation of the BST, with -1 for null nodes

examples.explanation: Inorder traversal visits: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 — the sorted order of all node values.
The BST has nodes 1, 2, 3, 4. Inorder traversal yields them in sorted order: [1, 2, 3, 4].

title: Range Sum of BST
description:
Given an integer array `tree` representing a **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in level-order (BFS) order, and two integers `low` and `high`, return the **sum of all node values** in the inclusive range `[low, high]`.

The array uses `-1` to represent `null` (missing) nodes. Node values will never be `-1`.

**Optimization:** Use the BST property to prune your search:
- If the current node's value is **less than** `low`, the answer lies entirely in the **right subtree**.
- If the current node's value is **greater than** `high`, the answer lies entirely in the **left subtree**.
- Otherwise, include the current node and explore both subtrees.

**Example:**
```
tree = [10, 5, 15, 3, 7, -1, 18]
low = 7, high = 15
```
Nodes in range [7, 15]: 7, 10, 15 → sum = `32`
constraints: 1 <= tree.length <= 10000
Node values are unique integers in the range [1, 10^5] (excluding -1, which denotes null)
The input is guaranteed to be a valid BST
Expected time complexity: O(n)
Expected auxiliary space: O(h) where h is the height of the BST

params.name: tree
params.description: Level-order (BFS) representation of the BST, with -1 for null nodes

params.name: low
params.description: The lower bound of the range (inclusive)

params.name: high
params.description: The upper bound of the range (inclusive)

examples.explanation: Nodes with values in [7, 15] are: 7, 10, 15. Their sum is 7 + 10 + 15 = 32.

title: Convert Sorted Array to Height-Balanced BST
description:
Given an integer array `nums` sorted in **ascending order**, convert it to a **height-balanced Binary Search Tree (BST)** and return the tree as a **level-order (BFS) integer array**.

A **height-balanced BST** is a BST where the depth of the two subtrees of every node never differs by more than 1.

Use `-1` to represent `null` nodes in your output. Trim any trailing `-1`s from the output array.

**Strategy:** Always choose the **middle element** of the current range as the root. This ensures balance.
- For `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]`, the middle element is `4` (index 3).
- Left half `[1, 2, 3]` → left subtree, right half `[5, 6, 7]` → right subtree.
- Recurse on each half.

**Example:**
```
nums = [-10, -3, 0, 5, 9]
```
Choose middle = `0` as root. Left half = `[-10, -3]`, right half = `[5, 9]`.
Result: `[0, -3, 9, -10, -1, 5, -1]`

**Note:** When there are two middle elements (even-length array), choose the **lower middle** (index `(len-1)/2`). If your approach produces a different valid balanced BST, it will also be accepted as long as it is balanced and a valid BST.
constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 10000
nums is sorted in strictly ascending order
Node values are integers in the range [-10^4, 10^4]
Expected time complexity: O(n)
Expected auxiliary space: O(n)

params.name: nums
params.description: A sorted integer array in ascending order to be converted into a height-balanced BST

examples.explanation: Middle of [-10,-3,0,5,9] is 0 (index 2). Left = [-10,-3], right = [5,9]. Middle of [-10,-3] is -3 (index 1, lower mid). Middle of [5,9] is 9 (index 1, lower mid). Result tree in level-order: [0, -3, 9, -10, -1, 5, -1].
Middle element is 2 (index 1). Left = [1], right = [3]. The resulting balanced BST in level-order is [2, 1, 3].
